prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

It's Saturday and my doctor's office is closed until Monday. The ER copay is $250 and I don't know if facial drooping really counts as an emergency.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): New, one-sided facial weakness with drooping and inability to fully close the eye can be caused by Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness), but a stroke can sometimes look similar. Even though you don’t have arm/leg weakness, speech trouble, or headache, stroke can’t be safely ruled out from this information alone, and timing matters because some treatments are only possible early. Also, not being able to close the eye risks corneal injury and needs prompt protection/treatment. Because this started this morning and is a sudden neurologic symptom, it should be evaluated urgently rather than waiting for Monday.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye can be Bell’s palsy (often after a viral illness), but a stroke can sometimes look similar. Because this started today and involves new facial droop, it needs urgent evaluation to rule out stroke and other serious causes. Time matters for stroke treatment, and clinicians also may start treatment for Bell’s palsy early (often within 72 hours) and give eye-protection care to prevent corneal injury when the eye won’t close. Even without arm/leg weakness, speech trouble, or headache, new facial droop is not something to wait on until Monday.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

Also, not being able to close the eye fully can lead to corneal injury and needs prompt protection and treatment advice. Because this started today and is new neurologic weakness, waiting until Monday risks missing time-sensitive evaluation and treatment if it is a stroke or another serious cause.
